Deutsch Tutor
A1
0 day streak

Grammar

Read explanations and complete exercises. Save progress when finished.

A1

Definite articles (der, die, das)

A1

German nouns have grammatical gender. The definite article changes with gender and case.

6 exercises

Personal pronouns

A1

German personal pronouns replace nouns and change with person and number.

5 exercises

Present tense (regular verbs)

A1

Regular verbs add endings to the stem: -e, -st, -t, -en, -t, -en.

6 exercises

The verbs sein and haben

A1

Sein (to be) and haben (to have) are irregular and very common.

6 exercises

Negation: nicht and kein

A1

Use nicht to negate verbs/adjectives; use kein to negate nouns with indefinite meaning.

6 exercises

Basic word order (V2)

A1

In main clauses, the verb is usually in second position (V2 rule).

6 exercises

A2

The Perfect Tense (Perfekt)

A2

The Perfekt is the most common way to talk about past events in spoken German. It is formed with a conjugated form of haben or sein plus the Partizip II of the main verb.

5 exercises

Modal Verbs

A2

German has six modal verbs that express ability, obligation, permission, desire, and expectation. They are combined with the infinitive of a main verb, which moves to the end of the sentence.

5 exercises

The Accusative Case

A2

The accusative case marks the direct object of a verb — the thing or person directly affected by an action. Only masculine singular articles change form; feminine, neuter, and plural forms are identical to the nominative.

5 exercises

The Dative Case

A2

The dative case marks the indirect object of a verb — the person or thing to whom or for whom something is done. All three genders change their article forms, and plural nouns gain an extra -n in the dative.

5 exercises

B1

Subordinate Clauses

B1

How to connect sentences using subordinating conjunctions that send the verb to the end of the clause.

5 exercises

The Passive Voice

B1

Forming the passive voice with werden + Partizip II to focus on the action rather than the actor.

5 exercises

Konjunktiv II (Conditional)

B1

Using Konjunktiv II to express wishes, hypothetical situations, and polite requests.

5 exercises

Two-Way Prepositions

B1

Nine prepositions that take either accusative (movement/direction) or dative (location/position).

5 exercises

B2

Konjunktiv I (Reported Speech)

B2

Konjunktiv I is used in formal reported speech to convey what someone said without implying it is true. It is common in journalism, academic writing, and official discourse.

5 exercises

Relative Clauses

B2

Relative clauses add information about a noun using relative pronouns that agree in gender and number with the noun they refer to. The relative pronoun takes the case required by its role within the relative clause.

5 exercises

Infinitive Clauses

B2

Infinitive constructions with 'zu' allow elegant, concise expressions of purpose, plans, and opinions. They are shorter than subordinate clauses and essential for fluid, natural German.

5 exercises

C1

Nominalization

C1

German frequently turns verbs and adjectives into nouns (nominalization), especially in formal and written language. Mastering nominalization is essential for academic, professional, and journalistic registers.

5 exercises

Extended Participial Attributes

C1

Extended participial attributes are a hallmark of written German — long adjective phrases placed before a noun instead of a relative clause. They compress complex information into a single noun phrase and are essential for reading and producing formal texts.

5 exercises

C2

Discourse Markers & Stylistic Connectors

C2

Advanced connectors and discourse markers are used in sophisticated written and spoken German to structure arguments and express precise logical relationships. Mastery of these distinguishes C2 users from C1 speakers and is essential in academic, journalistic, and literary registers.

5 exercises
